title: Energy and Metabolism
text: Metabolism is the full set of chemical reactions that manage energy and matter in cells. This section introduces anabolic and catabolic pathways, activation energy, enzymes, and ATP as a reusable energy currency.

title: Enzymes
text: Enzymes lower activation energy by binding substrates at active sites. The section covers induced fit, cofactors, inhibitors, allosteric regulation, and how temperature and pH affect enzyme activity.

title: ATP and Energy Coupling
text: ATP couples exergonic and endergonic reactions. Hydrolysis of phosphate bonds can power transport, motion, and biosynthesis, while cellular respiration regenerates ATP from ADP and phosphate.

title: Energy in Living Systems
text: Cellular respiration extracts energy from glucose by transferring electrons through redox reactions. The chapter connects glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

title: Glycolysis
text: Glycolysis splits glucose into two pyruvate molecules in the cytoplasm. It includes an energy investment phase, an energy payoff phase, substrate-level phosphorylation, ATP production, and NADH formation.

title: Oxidation of Pyruvate and the Citric Acid Cycle
text: Pyruvate enters mitochondria, becomes acetyl CoA, and feeds the citric acid cycle. The Krebs cycle releases carbon dioxide and loads electron carriers NADH and FADH2 for later ATP production.

title: Oxidative Phosphorylation
text: Oxidative phosphorylation uses the electron transport chain and chemiosmosis. Electron carriers donate energy to pump protons, creating a gradient that drives ATP synthase.

title: Metabolism without Oxygen
text: Fermentation and anaerobic respiration allow ATP production when oxygen is limited. The section compares lactic acid fermentation, alcohol fermentation, and alternative terminal electron acceptors.

title: Overview of Photosynthesis
text: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy stored in sugars. Chloroplasts, pigments, carbon dioxide, water, oxygen release, and the relationship between light reactions and the Calvin cycle are introduced.

title: The Light-Dependent Reactions of Photosynthesis
text: Light-dependent reactions occur in thylakoid membranes. Photosystems absorb photons, split water, release oxygen, move electrons, pump protons, and generate ATP and NADPH.

title: Using Light Energy to Make Organic Molecules
text: The Calvin cycle fixes carbon dioxide into carbohydrate using ATP and NADPH. The section explains carbon fixation, reduction, regeneration of RuBP, and the role of rubisco.

title: The Cell Cycle
text: The cell cycle includes interphase, mitotic phase, and cytokinesis. G1, S, and G2 prepare a cell for division by growth, DNA replication, and checkpoint control.

title: Control of the Cell Cycle
text: Cyclins, cyclin-dependent kinases, and checkpoints regulate cell-cycle progression. Failure of checkpoint control can lead to uncontrolled cell division and cancer.

title: Cancer and the Cell Cycle
text: Cancer can result when mutations disrupt normal cell-cycle regulation. Proto-oncogenes, tumor suppressor genes, apoptosis, and metastasis explain how tumors develop and spread.

title: The Process of Meiosis
text: Meiosis creates haploid gametes through two divisions after one round of DNA replication. Homologous chromosomes pair, crossing over occurs, and independent assortment increases genetic variation.

title: Mendel's Experiments and the Laws of Probability
text: Mendel used pea plants to infer inheritance patterns. Dominant and recessive traits, alleles, segregation, Punnett squares, and probability help predict offspring genotypes and phenotypes.

title: Historical Basis of Modern Understanding
text: Experiments by Griffith, Avery, Hershey, Chase, Franklin, Watson, and Crick established DNA as genetic material and supported the double-helix model.

title: Basics of DNA Replication
text: DNA replication is semiconservative. Helicase unwinds the double helix, primase lays primers, DNA polymerase extends new strands, and ligase joins Okazaki fragments.

title: The Genetic Code
text: The genetic code maps mRNA codons to amino acids. Start codons, stop codons, reading frames, redundancy, and translation connect nucleotide sequence to protein sequence.

title: Prokaryotic Transcription
text: Transcription produces RNA from a DNA template. RNA polymerase, promoters, initiation, elongation, termination, and mRNA processing explain how gene information is copied.

title: The Mole and Avogadro's Number
text: The mole connects particle counts to measurable amounts of substance. Avogadro's number, molar mass, formula units, and unit conversions are used to move between grams, moles, and particles.

title: Reaction Stoichiometry
text: Balanced chemical equations provide mole ratios for reactants and products. Stoichiometry problems use coefficients, limiting reactants, theoretical yield, and percent yield.

title: Relative Strengths of Acids and Bases
text: Acid and base strength depends on equilibrium behavior in water. The section compares strong acids, weak acids, conjugate bases, ionization constants, and Bronsted-Lowry proton transfer.

title: Newton's Second Law of Motion
text: Newton's second law relates net force, mass, and acceleration. Free-body diagrams, systems, units of newtons, and the equation F equals m times a support quantitative dynamics problems.

title: Real Numbers and Problem Solving
text: Mathematics problem solving uses real numbers, variables, expressions, equations, and careful step-by-step reasoning. This section is useful for students who find math hard and need algebra foundations.

title: Functions and Function Notation
text: Functions describe relationships between inputs and outputs. Function notation, domain, range, graphs, tables, and equations help organize mathematical models.

title: Defining the Derivative
text: Derivatives measure instantaneous rate of change and slopes of tangent lines. Limits, difference quotients, velocity, and graphical interpretation are core ideas in calculus.
